skill.name: pptx-generator
skill.description: Generate, edit, and inspect PowerPoint presentations with PptxGenJS, markitdown, template XML workflows, design-system guidance, and slide QA practices.
skill.summary: Create, edit, and review PPTX decks using guided PowerPoint generation workflows.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are false positives from markdown examples, color values, XML snippets, and presentation terminology. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malware, or command-and-control behavior. The remaining risk is medium because the skill asks users to install dependencies, manipulate user-provided PPTX archives, and run locally generated JavaScript.

medium_findings.title: Local JavaScript Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to create slide JavaScript files, dynamically require them, and run a Node compile script. This is legitimate for PptxGenJS deck generation, but unreviewed or externally supplied slide modules could execute arbitrary local code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic require loop and Node execution command are explicit. Risk depends on whether generated or imported slide files are reviewed before execution.

medium_findings.title: User-Provided PPTX Archive Extraction Needs Path Controls
medium_findings.description: The template workflow copies a user-provided PPTX and directs extraction into an editable XML tree with Python zipfile. The documentation does not state path normalization requirements, so generated helper code should avoid unsafe extraction of crafted archive entries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly handles user-provided PPTX archives, but no exact extraction code is present. This is a semantic risk requiring cautious implementation.

medium_findings.title: Global Dependency Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends pip and global npm installation commands for markitdown, PptxGenJS, React, and sharp. These are expected dependencies, but global installs increase supply-chain and environment-change risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install commands are explicit and legitimate for the stated workflow. The risk is environmental and supply-chain related, not evidence of malware.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Matches Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, C2 keyword, and reconnaissance alerts are caused by markdown tables, code examples, color values, XML ellipses, and presentation terminology. No evidence found of malware, credential theft, command-and-control behavior, or prompt injection text in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible documentation content rather than executable malicious logic. The confidence is high because the reviewed context explains the pattern matches.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example.com image, background, and hyperlink examples in PptxGenJS documentation. They are not exfiltration endpoints, but generated decks can reference remote assets if the user chooses to use them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly placeholders in API examples. There is no evidence of credentials, tracking endpoints, or suspicious domains.

low_findings.title: Temporary Directory Use Is Operational Guidance
low_findings.description: The /tmp instruction is presented as a workaround for zipfile seek behavior on mounted volumes. No evidence found that it is used for persistence, hiding files, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local context gives a benign reason for temporary output. The evidence does not show stealth or unauthorized file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local JavaScript Execution Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to create slide JavaScript files, dynamically require them, and run a Node compile script. This is legitimate for PptxGenJS deck generation, but unreviewed or externally supplied slide modules could execute arbitrary local code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic require loop and Node execution command are explicit. Risk depends on whether generated or imported slide files are reviewed before execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Provided PPTX Archive Extraction Needs Path Controls
dangerous_patterns.description: The template workflow copies a user-provided PPTX and directs extraction into an editable XML tree with Python zipfile. The documentation does not state path normalization requirements, so generated helper code should avoid unsafe extraction of crafted archive entries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly handles user-provided PPTX archives, but no exact extraction code is present. This is a semantic risk requiring cautious implementation.

content.value_statement: Building consistent PowerPoint decks is slow when layout, styling, and QA are handled manually. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PPTX creation, editing, extraction, and review workflows.
actual_capabilities: Creates new PPTX decks with PptxGenJS slide modules and a compile workflow.
Reads presentation text through markitdown for analysis and quality checks.
Edits existing PPTX templates by unpacking and modifying slide XML content.
Provides slide-type guidance for cover, table of contents, section, content, and closing slides.
Applies design-system guidance for palettes, typography, spacing, page badges, and visual hierarchy.
Documents QA loops for preview slides, extracted text checks, placeholders, and formatting issues.
limitations: Requires local dependencies such as markitdown, PptxGenJS, Node.js, and optional icon packages.
Generated JavaScript should be reviewed before running because it executes locally.
Template editing can break PPTX structure if XML relationships are changed incorrectly.
Remote images or hyperlinks must be supplied and approved by the user.

use_cases.title: Prepare client decks
use_cases.description: Turn an outline, brand palette, and source notes into a structured PowerPoint deck with varied slide layouts.

use_cases.title: Refresh an existing template
use_cases.description: Copy a user-provided PPTX, map new content to template slides, and update slide XML while preserving formatting.

use_cases.title: Audit presentation content
use_cases.description: Extract text from a PPTX and check for missing content, placeholder text, slide order, and page badges.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with a clear topic and audience.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 6-slide PowerPoint deck about [topic] for [audience]. Include a cover, agenda, three content slides, and a summary slide.

prompt_templates.title: Use a specific design direction
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a PowerPoint deck from this outline using a professional palette, varied slide layouts, page badges, and left-aligned body text.

prompt_templates.title: Edit a template deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this PPTX as a template. Replace the placeholder content with the attached source material, preserve layout quality, and output an edited deck.

prompt_templates.title: Generate and verify a full deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create the slide modules, compile the PPTX, extract text with markitdown, list any issues found, fix them, and verify the affected slides again.

output_examples.input: A request for an 8-slide investor update deck.
output_examples.output: A complete PPTX with a cover slide, agenda, metrics slides, roadmap slide, risk slide, and closing slide.

output_examples.input: A user-provided template deck and new campaign copy.
output_examples.output: An edited presentation that keeps the template structure while replacing text, removing unused placeholders, and preserving visual hierarchy.

output_examples.input: A finished presentation that needs review.
output_examples.output: A QA summary covering extracted text, missing content, placeholder checks, slide order, page badges, and recommended fixes.

best_practices: Review generated JavaScript before running Node commands on your machine.
Keep source decks and generated outputs in separate folders to protect originals.
Run text extraction and at least one fix-and-verify cycle before sharing a deck.
anti_patterns: Running generated slide modules from untrusted sources without inspection.
Editing PPTX XML relationships without updating presentation references.
Using the same text-heavy slide layout throughout a deck.

faq.question: Can this skill create a PPTX from scratch?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides creation of PptxGenJS slide modules and a compile script for the final deck.

faq.question: Can it edit an existing presentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a template workflow that copies a PPTX, extracts XML, edits slide content, and repacks the deck.

faq.question: Does it require installed software?
faq.answer: Yes. It expects tools such as Node.js, PptxGenJS, Python, and markitdown for key workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe to run generated code automatically?
faq.answer: No. Review generated JavaScript and dependencies before running local commands.

faq.question: Can it inspect slide text?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses markitdown examples to extract text for review and quality checks.

faq.question: Does it guarantee exact PowerPoint rendering?
faq.answer: No. Rendering may vary by PowerPoint version, fonts, images, and viewer compatibility.
